serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of mala chicken is approximately 200g, representing a single-person main dish portion as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres and zi char stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Mala Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mala Chicken (cr_id F11020042). Saturated fat and sugar values estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue (fried spiced chicken, USDA SR Legacy #05209) where HPB does not report these fields, as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed fried chicken with vegetables and spice coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mala chicken. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for fried chicken with mixed coating and vegetables (GI ~30–40 range). The low carbohydrate content (7.0g/100g) and high protein and fat content suppress postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (35 × 7.0 × 2) / 100 = 4.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 850mg per 100g — well above the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit mala chicken due to its very high sodium content (850mg/100g), driven by mala spice paste, soy sauce, and added salt; if consumed, request reduced seasoning and avoid the sauce to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (7.0g/100g) and high protein (15.3g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but high fat content (12.2g/100g) and deep-frying method warrant portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mala chicken has a low estimated GI (~35) and moderate carbohydrate load, making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions; however, the high fat content from deep-frying may contribute to insulin resistance over time and should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (850mg/100g), saturated fat (~3.2g/100g), and deep-fried preparation elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mala chicken poses a high cardiovascular concern due to its combination of very high sodium, saturated fat from deep-frying, and potential trans fat from commercial cooking oils; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and prefer grilled or steamed chicken alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 850mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g RA threshold; dried chilli (nightshade) may trigger inflammatory responses
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mala chicken is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its very high sodium content worsening joint swelling and the presence of dried chillies (nightshade family) which may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mala Chicken (麻辣鸡, Ayam Mala) is a popular Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of deep-fried chicken pieces tossed with dried chillies, cashew nuts, garlic, ginger, and a mala (numbing-spicy) spice paste derived from Sichuan culinary tradition. The name 'mala' (麻辣) refers to the characteristic combination of numbing (má, from Sichuan peppercorn) and spicy (là, from dried chilli) flavours. The dish is typically prepared by marinating chicken pieces, deep-frying until golden and crispy, then wok-tossing with mala paste, soy sauce, dried chillies, and cashew nuts. The result is a dish with a crispy exterior, juicy interior, and an intensely aromatic, spicy-numbing flavour profile. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 200 kcal, 15.3g protein, 12.2g fat, and 7.0g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 850mg driven by the mala paste and soy sauce. The low estimated GI (~35) and high protein content make it relatively suitable for blood glucose management, but the very high sodium makes it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. It is commonly served as a main dish or sharing plate at Singapore hawker centres, zi char stalls, and casual dining restaurants, and is widely popular as a snack or meal accompaniment. The dish is halal-certified in many establishments. Cashew nuts contribute tree nut allergen risk; soy sauce contributes soy and gluten allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mala chicken?
Is mala chicken high in sodium?
What is mala chicken made of?
Can I eat mala chicken if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between mala chicken and regular fried chicken?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11020042, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, sugar, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue (fried spiced chicken) and documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for fried chicken with coating.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.8g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.9g/100g), trans_fat_g (0.1g/100g), sugar_g (2.5g/100g), potassium_mg (210mg/100g), and cholesterol_mg (65mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken (USDA #05209, 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter') as HPB Foodo does not report these fields for this entry. These values are approximate and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis of the commercial preparation. 2. Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry, and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue was available to provide defensible estimates for all fields; limited published micronutrient data exists for this specific mala chicken preparation. 3. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database range for fried chicken with mixed coating; no direct GI measurement for mala chicken was found in approved sources. 4. The dietary tag 'plant_based' present in the input record has been removed as mala chicken is a meat-based dish and this tag was erroneous. 5. Halal status retained from input as commonly applicable to hawker-prepared mala chicken in Singapore, but individual establishment certification should be verified.
